speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is May 31, 1985. Survivor Rose Schachter. Interviewer Rhoda Daum-Kenner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue the story. You were hiding. The Russian planes were shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came so low, you could see the pilots. You could see the machine guns. Until today, I don't understand why were they shooting at the civilians. I assume they were shooting at the Germans. They didn't know there were some others there between them. But I didn't like it. And it didn't take long. I remember, I even jumped into one of the trucks-- German trucks-- passing by to get away. And then it was a still stand. And there was no houses, nothing. We were in this big, big field someplace. And the news came through that the war is over. You could see the Germans left behind so many things. The one-- the soldiers which were running-- we found, all of a sudden, a barrel of butter. Didn't know what to do with that. Then the English came in. The Americans came in. The Americans came in. They took us with trucks to the nearest barracks, and they kept us there. They fed us there. And we were just waiting for some kind of transportation, which took maybe a month or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with the girls that you were working with in that-- in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Everybody got dispersed. Everybody ran different directions. I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still Yanina?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still Yanina, believe it or not. A month later, I didn't admit to anyone that I'm Jewish. Somehow I had the notion that I'm the only surviving Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: In all of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In all of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a month, I talk to a-- with hands, I didn't speak English-- but there was this American captain. Tried to make some conversation. I was scared to tell him I'm Jewish. The fear was in me for a long time. A month. [SIGHS] Then we waited for the transportation. And there were two groups that divide us, one which wanted to go to the west, like France, one which wanted to go to the east. In the beginning, I really don't know where to go to look for anybody from my relatives. But I decided the best bet maybe is to go to Ostrog back. Maybe I find somebody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I attached myself to a group of Russian women, 400 Russian girls, which were being repatriated from Germany back to Russia. So I figured it out. If they go to Russia-- just my own calculations-- they go to Russia, they have to pass near the border someplace. And if they pass the town Shepetovka, which was the other side of Ostrog, I'm OK. I'll go down, and I'll make my way to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it wasn't so simple as I thought because when we travelled, again, we traveled a couple of hours, and then we had to wait overnight for the next train. So while they-- we were waiting for the next train, they were-- they were-- the Russians NKVD, which is like not CIA. The police. The secret police. Police? Secret police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Secret police. Going around, pointing at certain people to come for interrogation because they considered those Russians all traitors. Imagine that from 400 women, he pointed at me. [LAUGHS] I was lucky. 400 women. I had a red sweater at that time. I remember, he said, the one in red. I had a small bundle with some belongings that I accumulated. I left. I took-- I went with this Russian. It was in the evening. And I went into this office. Not right away. First they tire you outside the door. Then I went in. And he started interro-- I took the papers with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yanina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He started interrogating me. So I told him. I said, I'm safe here. I thought to myself, I'm safe. I'm Jewish. So when I told him I'm Jewish, he got into such a fury with the fist on the table. If you are Jewish, the only thing is that you cooperated with the Germans because all Jews are dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I run from-- survived Hitler, I wind up almost in Siberia. I was crying. I showed him I was on false papers. For a long time, they sent me out, sitting on the floor, behind the doors. After a couple of hours, he called me back, start interrogating again. Well, finally, he believed me. Not only believed me, but he told me that he's Jewish. He was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So in the meantime, my transport left with my little belongings I had. They went ahead. So I was again left alone. I didn't have a ticket, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went with me. They bought me a ticket. Instead to buy to Shepetovka, they bought someplace else. The conductor didn't let me into the train. It's a good thing a couple of soldiers took my part, and they threatened the conductor. So he let me in. So I made my way to Ostrog. When I-- The conductor was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The conductor was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Russian trains already. When I came to Ostrog, there were three more Jews which came back that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: People that you knew from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I was-- even if I didn't know, we stuck together because we were so scared of the Ukrainians. We were terrible scared because they are really-- they were out to finish off the couple of Jews which were left. So after two days, we left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found nothing? Nobody in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From those people, I found that my brother was in the [NON-ENGLISH] army, that he's alive. That's all I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. Another thing. That there are some distant relatives in Krakow which are now in Germany. So I-- we decided to leave. It was dangerous to stay there. So we made--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house-- did you go back to your house? Was it still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrog? No, I didn't go anyplace. We were very, very afraid. We were afraid to go out on the street. After the war already. So we made-- we, we, we were able to take the train, and I made my way to Krakow. And I found these relatives, and I stayed with them. The-- you know, it's a family with a small baby, three years old, a year old that was there. Anyway. And in Krakow, I start finding out about other people. But from Krakow, I think-- I don't remember well. I think I took one trip to Konskie to find out what's what, and then also for two or three days. And I found out that my parents and my sister were gone. Came back, and they had a friend which was the head of the Berihah, so he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the Berihah?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Berihah is an under-- at that time, an underground organization which transported Jews from Europe to certain-- the destination was Israel. But certain groups were located in Czechoslovakia, in Italy. So we were, at the beginning, in Szczecin, in Poland. And we were transporting them to the Czech border.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were people who had survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A lot of them which came back also from Russia. Our mission was to put the-- take them till Czechoslovakian border. Over there, another group took over, took them to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually smuggling them into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Italy, they went with the illegal boat to Israel. It wasn't so simple, because in order to transport them to Prague, you had to pass a German border and a Russian border. And at each of those points, they used to-- I used-- last transport, I had 500 people. And they used to take them out from the train. We instructed them before. Everybody got a different name. And they were going as repatriated Germans, back to Germany. None of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah. German Jews. German Jews or German. It doesn't matter. They didn't know. But none of them really spoke a word of German. They all spoke all Polish or Russian. So we instructed them before we left, they should keep their mouth shut. I do the talking. I had the false list with names. They were-- they took the people out. They called the names. Everybody was supposed to say yes. And I remember, this last transport I had to Prague, and in Prague, the border was bought off with money. But then I had to return. I returned in a train, a Polish train, military that also we bought off the soldiers that at the borders, I remember, they put me in a big, big case like this. And they were sitting on top, and one was playing the harmonica or something, just to pass the border. They smuggled me, back. Anyway, I took a couple of trips. It was very, very dangerous because one of our coworkers winded up in Siberia because they caught him. He was a couple of years in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did this till-- when did I arrive to my sister? I heard from other people that my sister is in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The one which is now here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Frankfurt, Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was studying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What a reunion that must have been.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked maybe for a year in the Berihah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been a wonderful reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She-- all of those things, you could find out at that time, from mouth to mouth. Really, there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't that interesting, that you were able to do that? And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No communication, like here, by phone. It's hard to imagine for people which never went through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But people told other people about survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what they're doing sometimes? People left names that if somebody else comes, that so and so is alive. It's hard. Very hard to imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remarkable. And then what happened when you got to Frankfurt? How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still involved in the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Berihah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But from Frankfurt, I went-- I still was working in the Berihah. And then I went to-- I think it was a fishing boat. Myself, I went to Israel. At that time, still that was the last days, it was illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war? Before '48?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very shortly. Because the boat was really a fishing boat meant for maybe, I don't know, a couple of people. There were 300 of us. And anytime we were on the sea, if another boat approached us, then we all have to go down. Nobody could go on the deck. And I was-- in '47, probably, I arrived. End of '47, I arrived to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital, which is in [NON-ENGLISH]. I had no experience as a nurse, but the need was so big because at that time, the Israel was the first war, right away, with the Arabs. They were fighting in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put me as a nurse. They taught me how to give an injection. And I worked in this hospital. For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- that was what? '48? Two years, probably. Because in '51-- no. One year, because then I went to work on a boat, Israeli boat, which was also transporting-- legally. That was after '48. Legally transporting refugees from Europe to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work on that boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another year. And then from the boat, my sister was at that time already in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had kept in touch with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I left out some things. Wait a moment. After the war-- wait a moment. When I went from Israel, I went to-- went to France. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: On your way to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time I didn't know. No. Wait a minute. That was after Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: After. Oh, after Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Israel, I went to France. I lived there, very miserable because the French didn't let you work officially. They didn't give you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Papers? Working papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Papers to stay. Not-- if you didn't have papers to stay, you didn't have papers to work. So I was there with another Israeli girl. We were living in a really, really-- didn't have-- almost starving. Between friends, we took home, illegally, some finishing work to do, and they paid us peanuts. That's how we lived. From France, I went to Hanover, to my relatives that I met in Krakow. Over there, I worked with them. They had a business. I worked with them. And from Frankfurt, I went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning-- the first of-- 6th of May, in '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your present husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him through common friends. My sister's friends, which were Polish, knew my husband's parents. And my sister told his parents' friends that she has a sister which is available. So the friends said, we know of somebody who's available.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took my telephone number, gave it to my husband's father. His father gave it to Harry. He called me. At that time, I was working also in the hospital here, in Queens. And he called me, and we made a date, and it took off very easy. And how long before you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long was I-- I think, not even half a year or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you married, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: He proposed after six days. He said, I propose. He proposed after six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when we talk with him, we'll find out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the Bronx for 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to Teaneck and we've lived here since for 24 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do? You said you were working in a hospital in Queens.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started out in regular, routine hospital work, taking blood and doing chemistry, things like that. And then I specialized in histology, which is the study of tissue. And since then, I worked in histology. I worked in Columbia, and my last job was in Helen Hayes Research, in orthopedics.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister is still in America? Still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is-- that's the one which your superior noticed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years ago, in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a union with your brother while he was still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. We used to go to London every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So three of you survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we're going every year to see to the grave and things. We go every November.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a family over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He had a wife. And she's now in Canada. She had her own children , so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still go back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is well, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean, your sister goes with you? Or you just go with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We all-- on all the trips, we go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Well, we thank you very much for taking the time to talk with us and share your stories. Is there anything else that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to say that I admire very much Mr. Spielberg. He has my whole admiration for what he's doing to the memory of the six million. I mean, he really-- in my book, he should go down in history--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure he will.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as the greatest, greatest man. I don't know him personally. I wish I could tell him thank you for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we will certainly transmit that message to the office and be sure that he receives it from you. And I thank you very much for taking the time to talk with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. From on the upper-- how do you say that? Upper--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right-- the upper row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upper row from the left is my brother, younger brother, then his wife, then is my parents, my mother and my father. At the extreme is my sister-in-law, which was married to my older brother. I'm the lower row. I am, my friend from school, and kneeling is my youngest sister. In the middle, I don't know who that is. Must have been a neighbor or something. I really don't know. Has nothing to do with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I assume it was taken at Konskie. In our house in Konskie.

speaker: interviewer
text: No particular occasion, just a photographer came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year? Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my gosh. That must have been in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been-- wait a moment. Let me think. That was before there-- must have been at least in '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war? Before everything started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the one on the left side? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the upper left is my brother, Meyer. Then it's his wife, I forgot the name. Then it's my mother, Hannah. Then it's my father Abraham. And that is my sister-in-law, [? Asha. ?] Then it's me. And the other extreme of me is a friend from school. And kneeling down is my youngest sister, Paula, and the baby, I don't know who it is. From the left, it's me. My name is Rose. From the right is Harry, my dear husband. And we were married in end of July the 27, 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this your wedding picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wedding-- only one wedding picture. I don't have any other pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there another one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that-- where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in the Bronx, at the wedding. In a small, little-- we were married in the synagogue, a small, little synagogue. The rabbi was a friend of the Schachters. And the picture was taken by our friend at the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the permit for me to work, when I arrived to Rovno on my false papers. That's my first legal document as a Polish. Yanina Bukowska, which were my false papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a copy, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy. I gave the original a gift to the Holocaust Museum of New York a couple of years ago, and they Xeroxed it for me. This is a document to prove that I don't have lice. They spread us all with a white powder, which is, I think it was DDT, called today. And they wanted to make sure that I have no lice. And everybody went through it. They just had to make sure that everybody's clean. Never mind what they did other things, but everybody's supposed to be clean. This is a paper whereby I could travel from Rovno to Berlin, which my new boss, Erwin Hadrian made it for me, and which permitted me to go by train with him from Rovno to Berlin to be his maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you traveled with the other SS on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's when I traveled in the luxury train with all the other big shots, SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Maidala. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the copy of this is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The copy of this is also in the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean the original.

speaker: interviewee
text: The original is in the future Jewish Museum, the Holocaust Museum of New York. I hope they use it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure they will. It's rather unique. I don't think many people had your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately.
